questions.questionText: Your warehouse shipment-auditing agent, built on the Claude Agent SDK, calls `fetch_manifest`, `compare_records`, and `flag_discrepancy` in a loop. The loop terminates as soon as `flag_discrepancy` returns a success status. In production, 30% of manifests contain more than one discrepancy, and for those manifests the audit consistently records only the first discrepancy before stopping. What change fixes the premature termination?

options.A: Continue the loop while `stop_reason` is `"tool_use"` and terminate only when the model returns `"end_turn"`, regardless of which individual tools have succeeded along the way.
options.B: Have `flag_discrepancy` return a `remaining_discrepancies` count in its result and keep the loop running until that count reaches zero.
options.C: Add a system prompt instruction that the agent must output the phrase "AUDIT COMPLETE" only after flagging every discrepancy, and terminate the loop when that phrase appears.
options.D: Raise the loop's iteration cap so that manifests with multiple discrepancies have enough tool-call budget to flag all of them before the loop is cut off.

questions.explanation: The loop is treating one tool's success as task completion, but only the model knows whether its audit is finished; `stop_reason: "end_turn"` is the authoritative completion signal, and `"tool_use"` means keep going. Option B is programmatic, which is the right category, but it relocates termination knowledge into a tool that has no way to know how many discrepancies the model hasn't yet examined. Option C replaces one unreliable signal with another — parsing natural-language phrases for loop termination is a named anti-pattern because phrasing drifts across responses and prompt revisions. Option D misreads the failure: the 30% of multi-discrepancy audits are stopped by the success-status check, not by an exhausted iteration cap, so more budget changes nothing.

questions.questionText: A compliance agent drafts regulatory summaries after several tool calls. In about 4% of runs the final summary stops mid-sentence, yet the pipeline marks the task complete. Logs show those runs ended with `stop_reason: "max_tokens"`, and the loop's exit condition is "the response contains no `tool_use` blocks." What is the correct fix?

options.A: Treat only `stop_reason: "end_turn"` as completion; when the loop sees `"max_tokens"`, continue the conversation so the model can finish the truncated summary.
options.B: Raise `max_tokens` to the model's maximum for the summarization call, which removes the possibility of the response being cut off mid-sentence.
options.C: Detect truncation by checking whether the response's final text block ends in terminal punctuation, and retry the request whenever it does not.
options.D: When the loop sees `stop_reason: "max_tokens"`, discard the partial output and restart the task from the beginning with the original prompt.

questions.explanation: The exit condition conflates "no tool_use blocks" with "done" — a truncated response also contains no tool_use blocks, which is exactly why 4% of runs are marked complete mid-sentence; branching on `stop_reason` distinguishes genuine completion (`"end_turn"`) from truncation (`"max_tokens"`) and lets the model continue where it stopped. Option B shrinks the window but doesn't close it — long summaries can still hit the higher ceiling, and the loop would still misclassify those runs as complete. Option C is the natural-language-signal anti-pattern: a truncated summary can coincidentally end at a period, and a complete one can end without one. Option D diagnoses the stop reason correctly but recovers wastefully — restarting from scratch re-spends every prior token and, since nothing changed, may simply truncate again, whereas continuing the conversation completes the remaining portion only.

questions.questionText: A property-listing enrichment agent calls `geocode_address`, `fetch_school_ratings`, and `fetch_crime_stats` for each listing. To cut token spend, the developer's loop sends only the original instructions plus the single most recent tool result on each iteration, discarding earlier results. Listings that need all three lookups now trigger repeated calls to tools that already ran, and sessions average 11 tool calls where 3 should suffice. What change fixes this?

options.A: Accumulate the full conversation history across iterations — each assistant `tool_use` turn followed by its `tool_result` — so the model can see which lookups it has already completed and reason across their results.
options.B: Add a system prompt rule stating "Never call a tool you have already called for the current listing" so the model stops issuing redundant requests.
options.C: Deduplicate at the executor layer: when the model repeats a call with identical arguments, return the cached earlier result instead of re-executing the lookup against the backend.
options.D: Tighten the iteration cap to 5 tool calls per listing so redundant sessions are cut off early, capping the cost of the repeated lookups.

questions.explanation: The agentic loop depends on tool results persisting in conversation history; because each iteration exposes only the latest result, the model literally cannot know which lookups already ran, so it re-requests them — accumulating history restores that knowledge and drops sessions back toward 3 calls. Option B instructs the model to avoid something it has no information to detect: the record of prior calls was discarded, so the rule is unenforceable regardless of prompt strength. Option C cuts backend execution cost, which is real but partial — the model still sees only one result per iteration, so it still can't reason across the three datasets or recognize completion, and the 11-call loop persists. Option D treats the symptom by truncating it: listings needing all three lookups would now routinely terminate incomplete rather than cheaply.

questions.questionText: A trip-planning agent regularly emits a single assistant turn containing three `tool_use` blocks — `search_flights`, `search_hotels`, and `search_transfers`. Your loop executes only the first block and sends back one `tool_result`. The next API call then fails with a 400 error citing missing `tool_result` blocks. What is the correct handling?

options.A: Execute every `tool_use` block from the assistant turn and return one `tool_result` per `tool_use` ID, all inside the single next user message.
options.B: Return the flight result immediately, then supply the hotel and transfer results in two subsequent user messages as each remaining search completes.
options.C: Set `disable_parallel_tool_use` on the request's `tool_choice` so the model is constrained to requesting one tool per assistant turn.
options.D: Add a system prompt instruction telling the agent to call at most one tool per response so the loop's single-result assumption holds.

questions.explanation: Parallel tool use is valid API output: when an assistant turn contains multiple `tool_use` blocks, the immediately following user message must contain a matching `tool_result` for every block's ID, which is what option A implements — and the three independent searches then run in one round trip. Option B violates the protocol that produced the 400 in the first place: the results cannot be spread across later messages; they all belong in the next user message. Option C uses a real parameter and would deterministically stop the errors, but it fixes the model to accommodate a broken loop — turning one round trip into three for independent lookups and leaving the loop unable to handle valid output if the setting is ever removed. Option D is the probabilistic version of C: the model will sometimes still batch calls, and every time it does, the 400 returns.

questions.questionText: Your catalog-onboarding system uses a coordinator that delegates to three subagents: image tagging, description writing, and price validation. A teammate prototypes letting the price-validation subagent invoke the image-tagging subagent directly whenever it detects a photo/price mismatch, skipping the coordinator round trip and cutting that path's latency by 15%. How should you evaluate this proposal?

options.A: Reject it: direct subagent-to-subagent invocation fragments orchestration — the coordinator can no longer observe, sequence, or retry tagging work it never knew was delegated.
options.B: Approve it: the coordinator still receives both subagents' final outputs at the end of the run, so observability is preserved and the 15% latency gain comes at no architectural cost.
options.C: Reject it: routing the tagging output through the price agent's context roughly doubles token consumption compared with routing the same work through the coordinator.
options.D: Approve it on the condition that the price-validation subagent appends the full tagging transcript to its final report, restoring the coordinator's view of what happened.

questions.explanation: The hub-and-spoke pattern exists so one place holds delegation state: when subagents invoke each other directly, a failed or incomplete tagging call happens outside the coordinator's knowledge, so nothing can detect it mid-run, retry it, or account for it when sequencing the remaining work — a 15% latency gain on one path doesn't offset losing recovery over that path entirely. Option B mistakes receiving final outputs for orchestration: results arriving at the end doesn't help the coordinator manage failures while the run is in progress. Option C rejects for an inaccurate reason — coordinator-routed work also passes results through two agent contexts, so token cost is comparable; the real cost is control, not tokens. Option D is a near-miss: an appended transcript gives after-the-fact visibility, but reading about a delegation later is not the same as being able to sequence or recover it, and it bloats the price agent's report besides.

questions.questionText: A legal-review system analyzes 90-page merger agreements. The coordinator splits each document among three clause-analysis subagents by page range (1–30, 31–60, 61–90). In roughly 20% of runs, subagents misinterpret clauses that reference the 4-page defined-terms section at the front of the document — for example, page 70 uses "Material Adverse Effect" as defined on page 5, and the third subagent applies the plain-English meaning instead. What is the most effective fix?

options.A: Give every subagent the full 90-page document as context and instruct each one to analyze and report only on its assigned page range.
options.B: Decompose along self-contained semantic boundaries (articles or clause topics) instead of page ranges, and include the 4-page defined-terms section in every subagent's context.
options.C: Add a synthesis subagent that receives all three analyses and reconciles any conclusions that conflict with each other before the final report is produced.
options.D: Run the three subagents sequentially instead of in parallel, so each one can read the analyses produced by the subagents that ran before it.

questions.explanation: The failure is a decomposition that severs semantic dependencies: page ranges split content from the definitions it relies on. Option B fixes both halves — boundaries that keep dependent content together, plus the small shared dependency (4 pages) replicated into each subagent's context at negligible cost. Option A also restores correctness but at the wrong scale: it triples per-subagent input to 90 pages each when the actual shared dependency is only the 4-page definitions section, largely defeating the point of decomposing. Option C patches downstream — a synthesis pass can only reconcile conclusions the subagents surfaced, and a subagent that silently applied the plain-English meaning produces no conflict to detect. Option D sacrifices parallelism without solving the problem: the second and third subagents see prior analyses, not the defined-terms section, unless an earlier agent happened to quote it.

questions.questionText: An insurance-claims triage coordinator routes claims to specialist subagents (fraud screening, damage assessment, payout calculation), but performs damage assessment itself for claims it judges "simple," reasoning that a subagent round trip is wasteful. In long sessions, after roughly 40 claims, the coordinator's routing decisions degrade: it misroutes claim types it handled correctly earlier in the session. What architectural change best addresses this?

options.A: Enable context compaction on the coordinator's session so older claim analyses are summarized automatically once the context approaches its limit.
options.B: Replace the coordinator every 10 claims with a freshly spawned instance, handing off a summary of routing decisions made so far in the session.
options.C: Delegate every assessment — including simple ones — to subagents, keeping the coordinator's context reserved for orchestration state rather than analysis transcripts.
options.D: Add a system prompt instruction directing the coordinator to keep its own damage assessments brief so they consume less of the session's context.

questions.explanation: The coordinator is degrading because its context fills with damage-assessment working material — photos descriptions, line-item reasoning — that crowds out the orchestration state routing depends on; around claim 40, earlier routing patterns are effectively displaced. Option C fixes the cause: a thin coordinator whose context holds only task state scales with the number of claims, not the depth of each analysis. Option A treats the symptom — compaction buys more claims per session, but analysis transcripts still dilute the context, and lossy summaries of routing history are exactly what you don't want compacted. Option B is the same symptom-treatment with extra failure surface: the handoff summary must capture everything routing depends on, and the mixed-concern design that caused the bloat carries over to each new instance. Option D reduces the leak rate rather than plugging it — briefer assessments still accumulate, and it relies on probabilistic compliance besides.

questions.questionText: A restaurant-inspection platform generates compliance reports using three subagents: photo analysis, violation-history retrieval, and report writing. To maximize throughput, the coordinator spawns all three in parallel. Reports frequently ship missing photo findings, because the report writer starts before photo analysis — the slowest of the three at about 30 seconds — has returned. How should the coordinator schedule the subagents?

options.A: Keep all three parallel, but have the report writer retry in a loop, re-checking for photo findings every few seconds until they become available.
options.B: Run all three subagents strictly sequentially — photo analysis, then violation history, then report writing — so every dependency is guaranteed to be satisfied.
options.C: Keep all three parallel, and instruct the report writer to leave placeholder sections for photo findings that a second-pass subagent fills in once analysis completes.
options.D: Spawn photo analysis and violation-history retrieval in parallel, then invoke the report writer only after both have returned, passing it their outputs.

questions.explanation: Parallelism is for independent tasks; the report writer consumes the other two subagents' outputs, so it belongs after them. Option D preserves the real available concurrency — photo analysis and history retrieval don't depend on each other — while making the dependent step wait for its inputs, bounding total latency near the 30-second slowest branch. Option B is correct but over-serialized: it forfeits the parallelism between the two independent tasks and adds their durations for no benefit. Option A bolts a polling loop onto a scheduling problem — subagents don't share live state to poll, and even simulated via re-invocation it burns tokens waiting instead of just sequencing the dependency. Option C ships the race condition into the artifact: a placeholder-and-patch second pass adds a full extra invocation per report and risks stitched-in findings that contradict the surrounding text the writer produced without them.

questions.questionText: A payroll-migration coordinator needs a subagent to map fields between a legacy HR schema and a new provider's schema. The developer passes the coordinator's entire 60,000-token session transcript to the subagent — including lengthy vendor-contract negotiations unrelated to schema work. The subagent's mappings are correct but slow to produce, and it occasionally cites contract terms as if they were mapping constraints. What is the correct way to pass context here?

options.A: Build a focused task prompt for the subagent containing only the two schema definitions, the mapping requirements, and explicit acceptance criteria — not the coordinator's transcript.
options.B: Pass the full transcript but prepend an instruction telling the subagent to disregard any content not related to schema mapping when producing its output.
options.C: Summarize the 60,000-token transcript down to a few thousand tokens with a separate compression call, and pass the summary as the subagent's context.
options.D: Configure the subagent with a larger context window so the full transcript fits with ample headroom and no longer degrades its processing.

questions.explanation: Subagents start fresh, which is an advantage: the coordinator should curate exactly the inputs the task needs — schemas, requirements, success criteria — rather than exporting its own history. Option A eliminates both symptoms at the source: less input means faster processing, and absent contract text can't be miscited as a constraint. Option B leaves the distraction in context and asks the model to probabilistically ignore it — the observed miscitations are evidence that irrelevant content in context influences output regardless of instructions. Option C is better than the raw transcript but still the wrong operation: blanket compression of mostly irrelevant material may lossily mangle the schema details that actually matter while still carrying negotiation residue in. Option D solves a problem the scenario doesn't have — the 60,000 tokens already fit; the failure is relevance and dilution, not capacity.

questions.questionText: A retail-analytics coordinator spawns one inventory-audit subagent per store, all at once, for a 25-store chain. Under the account's rate limits — which comfortably sustain about 5 concurrent subagents — roughly 40% of the spawned subagents fail with 429 errors, and the nightly audit completes with gaps. How should the spawning be redesigned?

options.A: Switch to fully sequential spawning: run one store's audit to completion before starting the next, eliminating concurrent requests entirely.
options.B: Keep spawning all 25 at once, but add exponential-backoff retries to each subagent so that those receiving 429 responses eventually succeed.
options.C: Run the subagents through a bounded worker pool sized to the rate limit — about 5 concurrent — queuing the remaining stores until a slot frees up.
options.D: Ask the API provider for a rate-limit increase sized to 25 concurrent subagents, keeping the current spawn-everything-at-once design unchanged.

questions.explanation: The stated limit — about 5 sustainable concurrent subagents — is the load-bearing constraint: correct design spawns up to the limit and queues the rest, getting maximum safe parallelism (roughly 5× faster than sequential) with no 429 storm. Option A eliminates the errors but ignores the same number: running one at a time when 5 are sustainable makes the nightly audit about five times longer than necessary. Option B keeps the thundering herd and merely retries into it — backoff is a good companion to a pool, but as the sole fix the 25-way contention persists and completion time becomes erratic. Option D outsources the fix: even if granted, the design fails again at the next constraint change (a 40-store expansion, a tightened quota), whereas a pool adapts by changing one number.
